lessons.title: RPLS Exam Format and Study Strategy
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of the Texas RPLS jurisprudence examination and the role of the national (NCEES) versus Texas-specific components.
Identify the high-yield Texas jurisprudence topics most heavily tested and allocate study time accordingly.
Apply a citation-anchored study method that ties each doctrine to its controlling statute or case.
Recognize common exam traps unique to Texas land law.
key_takeaways: The Texas RPLS path layers a Texas state-specific jurisprudence/law exam on top of the national NCEES Principles & Practice of Surveying (PS) exam.

High-yield Texas jurisprudence clusters around land-grant history/GLO, dignity of calls and footsteps, adverse possession (CPRC Ch. 16), easements, water boundaries, recording acts, and TBPELS standards (22 TAC Ch. 138).
Study by anchoring every rule to a controlling authority: a code section or a named Texas case.
The most dangerous traps are the places where Texas departs from general common law — memorize those departures cold.

content.html:
What this review covers

This module is an exam-focused review for the Texas jurisprudence portion of the Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) examination. It is not a first introduction to the doctrines — it assumes you have already studied boundary law, property law, and Texas statutes — and instead drills the high-yield Texas rules, the controlling citations, and the trap answers that separate a passing score from a near miss.

The Texas surveyor credential is reached by passing the national NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam (the SIT path, reviewed in Modules 25–26), gaining experience, and then passing the national Principles and Practice of Surveying (PS) exam together with a Texas-specific state examination that tests Texas law and rules. This module targets that Texas legal/jurisprudence material: title history, boundary-conflict resolution, easements and adverse possession, water boundaries, recording, and the TBPELS standards of practice. Because Texas land law is genuinely distinctive, the state component rewards precise knowledge of where Texas departs from general common law.

Where the points are

Jurisprudence questions are conceptual and scenario-based, not computational. A typical item gives a short fact pattern — a conflicting deed call, a shared driveway, a coastal grant, a quitclaim in the chain — and asks which rule controls and why. The reliable way to earn those points is to pre-load the controlling authority for each doctrine so that, when you read the facts, the right citation surfaces automatically.

Allocate study time roughly in proportion to the tested clusters:

  • Land-grant history, sovereigns, the GLO, and units (vara/labor/league) — the foundation that explains why Texas has no PLSS grid.
  • Dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine (Stafford v. King) — the single most-tested boundary concept.
  • Senior/junior rights and sequential vs. simultaneous conveyances.
  • Adverse possession (CPRC Ch. 16) and easements (note Texas disfavors prescription).
  • Water boundaries (gradient boundary, 30-ft rule, Luttes, Open Beaches).
  • Recording acts (pure-notice state) and TBPELS standards (22 TAC Ch. 138).

content.text: Study method: for every doctrine, write a one-line flashcard of the form 'RULE — controlling authority — Texas twist.' Example: 'Bed of a navigable stream is State-owned; private title stops at the gradient boundary — Motl v. Boyd (1926) — Texas uses the computed gradient line, not the ordinary high-water mark.'

rows: Texas land was never federal public domain; no PLSS township-range grid
Intent and footsteps override the mechanical hierarchy
It is NOT in the Property Code; tax payment is required for the 5-yr track
Texas disfavors them; joint/shared use defeats exclusivity
Gradient boundary, not ordinary high-water mark
Texas is PURE NOTICE — BFP wins even if it records second or never
Ch. 663 was REPEALED — do not cite it in 2026

content.text: Texas differs (the master trap): in federal public-land states original title flows from the United States through the PLSS. In Texas, title flows from one of four sovereigns — Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, or the State of Texas — and Texas land was never federal public domain, so there is no township-range grid. Nearly every distinctive Texas rule below flows from this single fact.

content.html:
How to use the rest of this module

Each remaining lesson is a focused review of one cluster, built around accordion blocks that summarize the controlling cases, exam-tip callouts, and a closing list of key takeaways you can review the night before the test. Lesson 7 is a set of rigorous scenario problems; Lesson 8 collects exam-day tactics. Read actively: after each case accordion, close it and try to state the holding and the citation from memory before moving on. On the exam, the difference between two plausible answer choices is almost always a Texas-specific departure from the general rule, so spend your review energy on those departures rather than on the parts of the law that are nationally uniform.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The Texas RPLS credential adds a Texas state law/jurisprudence exam to the national NCEES PS exam.
Jurisprudence questions are conceptual and scenario-based; success comes from anchoring each rule to its controlling authority.
Allocate study time to the high-yield clusters: grant history/GLO, dignity of calls/footsteps, adverse possession, easements, water boundaries, recording, and TBPELS standards.
Focus your review on the points where Texas departs from general common law — that is where the trap answers live.

flashcards.definition: National NCEES Fundamentals (FS) and Principles & Practice (PS) of Surveying exams plus a Texas-specific state law/jurisprudence examination administered through TBPELS.

flashcards.term: The single idea behind most 'Texas differs' answers
flashcards.definition: Texas title traces to one of four sovereigns (Spain, Mexico, Republic, State); Texas land was never federal public domain, so there is no PLSS township-range grid — descriptions are metes-and-bounds keyed to an original GLO survey + abstract number.
A one-line card pairing each rule with its controlling statute or case and the Texas-specific twist, e.g., 'Pure-notice recording — Prop. Code §13.001 — BFP wins even recording second.'

lessons.title: Texas Land Grant History — Deep Review
learning_objectives: List the four sovereigns of the soil and the instruments that severed land from each.
Explain the significance of the 1845 annexation and Texas's retention of its public domain.
Describe the role and archives of the General Land Office (GLO) and the empresario colonization system.
Convert among Texas surveying units (vara, labor, league) and cite the statutory vara value.
key_takeaways: Four sovereigns issued Texas titles: Spain, Mexico (titles), the Republic of Texas, and the State of Texas (patents).
On annexation in 1845 Texas retained all its vacant and unappropriated public lands; Texas land was never federal public domain.
The GLO (founded 1836) is custodian of original grant records and still issues patents; the Spanish Collection holds the civil-law grants.
The vara is statutorily 33⅓ in (2.7778 ft); a labor ≈ 177.1 ac and a league/sitio ≈ 4,428 ac.

content.html:
The four sovereigns of the soil

Every Texas chain of title bottoms out in a grant from one of four sovereigns, in chronological order: Spain (1716–1821), Mexico (1821–1836), the Republic of Texas (1836–1845), and the State of Texas (1845–present). Spanish and Mexican grants were perfected by a title (porciones, town lots, leagues and labors); Republic and State grants are perfected by a patent. The instrument that severs land from the sovereign is the first link in the chain; every later deed runs from that first grantee.

Perfected Spanish and Mexican titles remain valid today and were protected when sovereignty changed — the Constitution of the Republic of Texas (1836) honored valid Spanish/Mexican grants, and the United States recognized valid Mexican-era titles in the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848). Land already conveyed by a perfected Spanish or Mexican title cannot be re-patented, because sovereignty over that soil was already severed. This is why many South and Central Texas chains end not in a State patent but in a colonial league-and-labor grant.

content.text: Texas differs: In federal public-land states, original title flows from the United States (federal GLO, Homestead Act, PLSS). In Texas it flows from the State, and Texas land was never federal public domain — so there is no township-range PLSS grid. Texas grants are metes-and-bounds tracts keyed to an original survey name + abstract number.

content.html:
Annexation 1845: Texas keeps its land

Texas was an independent republic from 1836 to 1845. The annexation terms are the most consequential fact in Texas land law: under the Joint Resolution for Annexing Texas (1845), Texas "shall also retain all the vacant and unappropriated lands lying within its limits." The federal government did not take the public domain. Texas kept well over 200 million acres, administered by the General Land Office (GLO), and dedicated the land revenue to the Permanent School Fund (and later the Permanent University Fund).

For the exam, the practical consequences are: (1) Texas has very little federal land; (2) the original grant in any Texas chain is a State or pre-statehood grant, never a federal patent; and (3) the apex custodian of grant records is a state agency, the GLO, not the federal BLM.

sections.title: The General Land Office (GLO)
sections.content: Founded December 22, 1836 by the First Congress of the Republic — the oldest existing Texas state agency. It maintains land records, produces official maps and surveys, and issues patents. Its archive holds roughly 35.5 million land-grant records (back to 1720) and about 80,000 maps; the Spanish Collection is the depository for roughly 4,200 Spanish/Mexican grants (about 26 million acres). South Texas Spanish/Mexican grants underwent a legislative confirmation/validation process around 1850–1852, after which the GLO issued confirming patents. Statutory link: Tex. Nat. Res. Code Ch. 31; §31.001 defines the 'Land Office' as the GLO.

sections.title: The empresario colonization system (Mexican Texas)
sections.content: Under Mexican rule (1821–1836), empresarios contracted to settle a set number of families in exchange for land (roughly 5 leagues + 5 labores per 100 families). Settlers had to convert to Catholicism and swear allegiance to Mexico. Stephen F. Austin inherited his father's 1821 contract (the 'Old Three Hundred') and settled 1,500+ families; about forty-one empresario grants were made 1821–1835. Framework: Mexican National Colonization Law of 1824 and the State Colonization Law of Coahuila y Tejas (1825). This is a civil-law mechanism with no English common-law equivalent — land distributed by a state-licensed contractor under public-law conditions, not private-market conveyance.

content.html:
Texas surveying units: vara, labor, league

Because Texas inherited the Spanish/Mexican system, original field notes are often in varas. Texas law fixed the vara at exactly 33⅓ inches (2.7778 ft). Memorize the conversions: 36 varas = exactly 100 ft; feet × 0.36 = varas; varas × 2.7778 = feet. Area units key to land use: a labor (1,000,000 sq varas, 1,000 varas square) is about 177.1 acres of farmland; a league/sitio (25,000,000 sq varas, 5,000 varas square) is about 4,428 acres of grazing land and equals 25 labors. The standard married-head-of-family grant under the colonization laws was a league + labor ≈ 4,605.5 acres; a single man received one-third league (≈ 1,476 acres).

content.text: Exam tip: a vara conversion is a common numeric stem even on a 'jurisprudence' exam. Remember 950 vrs × 2.7778 ≈ 2,638.9 ft, and that historic varas varied (Spanish ~32.9 in, Mexican ~32.99 in) — but Texas statutorily uses 33⅓ in, so do not apply a foreign vara value to a Texas grant.

content.alt: Timeline of the four Texas sovereigns from Spain through the State of Texas with the instruments each used
content.caption: Figure: The four sovereigns of the soil and their granting instruments (title vs. patent).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Four sovereigns: Spain and Mexico issued titles; the Republic and State of Texas issue patents — and a perfected colonial title cannot be re-patented.
Annexation (1845): Texas retained its public domain, so its land was never federal public domain and there is no PLSS grid.
The GLO (1836) is the state custodian of grant records and still issues patents; the Spanish Collection holds the civil-law grants.
Vara = 33⅓ in = 2.7778 ft; labor ≈ 177.1 ac; league ≈ 4,428 ac; family grant (league + labor) ≈ 4,605.5 ac.

flashcards.definition: Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, and the State of Texas — the only sources of original Texas land title.
Texas retained all vacant and unappropriated public lands (Joint Resolution, 1845); Texas land was never federal public domain, and there is no federal PLSS grid in Texas.
33⅓ inches = 2.7778 ft. 36 varas = exactly 100 ft; feet × 0.36 = varas.
Labor = 1,000,000 sq varas ≈ 177.1 ac (farmland); league/sitio = 25,000,000 sq varas ≈ 4,428 ac (grazing) = 25 labors.

lessons.title: Property Law and Estates Review
learning_objectives: Distinguish the estates in land recognized in Texas and the presumption favoring fee simple.
Compare general warranty, special warranty, and quitclaim deeds and the implied covenants Texas recognizes.
Explain the Texas quitclaim/BFP rule and the 2021 four-year cleansing window (§13.006).
Apply the bona fide purchaser doctrine, including possession as constructive notice.
key_takeaways: Texas presumes a conveyance passes fee simple unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (Prop. Code §5.001).
A quitclaim conveys only whatever interest the grantor has, is not a muniment of title, and its grantee is generally not a BFP.
Prop. Code §13.006 (eff. 2021) gives a recorded quitclaim a four-year cleansing window for downstream purchasers — teach the old and new rules together.
Possession imparts constructive notice only if it is visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal (Madison v. Gordon).

content.html:
Estates in land

Texas recognizes the standard hierarchy of estates: fee simple absolute (the largest estate); defeasible fees — fee simple determinable ('so long as,' 'until,' leaving a possibility of reverter) and fee simple subject to condition subsequent ('but if,' 'provided that,' leaving a right of re-entry); the life estate (the life tenant owes a no-waste duty); and the future interests of remainder (in a third party) and reversion (retained by the grantor). The key Texas presumption: a conveyance is presumed to pass the entire fee simple unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (Prop. Code §5.001).

Texas has also abolished several archaic common-law rules by statute (Prop. Code §5.042, eff. 1984): the Rule in Shelley's Case, the doctrine of worthier title, and the rule forbidding a remainder to the grantor's heirs are abolished, and such limitations are given effect according to the conveyor's intent.

content.text: Texas differs / common trap: §5.042 does NOT abolish the doctrine of destructibility of contingent remainders — that doctrine is not listed in the statute. Texas rejects destructibility as a matter of case law (honoring grantor intent), not via §5.042. Do not attribute destructibility to §5.042 on the exam.

content.html:
Deeds and implied covenants

Three deed types recur on the exam. A general warranty deed warrants title against all defects, back through the entire chain. A special warranty deed warrants only against defects arising 'by, through, or under' the grantor. A quitclaim deed conveys only whatever interest, if any, the grantor has; it carries no warranty and is not a muniment of title.

The Texas wrinkle is in the implied covenants. At common law, 'grant' implied a broad covenant of seisin — that the grantor actually owns the estate. Prop. Code §5.023 deliberately narrows the implied covenants of 'grant'/'convey' to just two: (1) the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate to another, and (2) the estate is free from encumbrances. It does not imply that the grantor actually owns the property. The general warranty in Texas comes from the express 'warrant and forever defend' language of the statutory form (Prop. Code §5.022), not from the word 'grant.'

sections.title: Case: Geodyne Energy Income Prod. P'ship I-E v. Newton Corp., 161 S.W.3d 482 (Tex. 2005)
sections.content: 'A warranty deed to land conveys property; a quitclaim deed conveys the grantor's rights in that property, if any.' Whether an instrument is a quitclaim turns on the granting language taken as a whole, not on the label. If the instrument purports to convey the property itself, BFP protection may be available; if it conveys only the grantor's right, title, and interest, it is a quitclaim. Compare Cook v. Smith, 174 S.W. 1094 (Tex. 1915) (the 'quitclaim' label is not conclusive; examine the whole instrument), and Rogers v. Ricane Enters., 884 S.W.2d 763 (Tex. 1994) (a quitclaim is not a conveyance or a muniment of title).

sections.title: Case: Woodward v. Ortiz, 237 S.W.2d 286 (Tex. 1951)
sections.content: A grantee under a quitclaim (and successors) is generally NOT a bona fide purchaser: the quitclaim is treated as notice that the grantor's title may be defective, so the grantee takes subject to prior outstanding claims even if value was paid without actual notice. Foundational companion: Houston Oil Co. of Texas v. Niles, 255 S.W. 604 (Tex. Comm'n App. 1923).

content.text: Critical 2026 update — the quitclaim/BFP rule has been statutorily softened. Prop. Code §13.006 (eff. Sept. 1, 2021): after the fourth anniversary of recording, a quitclaim deed does not affect the good faith of a subsequent purchaser/creditor and is not notice of any unrecorded conveyance or encumbrance. So for quitclaims recorded on/after Sept. 1, 2021 there is a four-year cleansing window after which the quitclaim no longer destroys BFP status for downstream purchasers. The classic rule still applies to the immediate grantee and during the first four years — teach the two together.

content.html:
Bona fide purchaser doctrine

A bona fide purchaser (BFP) takes free of a prior unrecorded interest only if it shows (1) valuable consideration (not nominal; a gift or quitclaim-only taker generally fails); (2) good faith; and (3) without notice, neither actual nor constructive. Constructive notice arises from the records and from possession of the land.

The leading Texas authority on possession-as-notice is Madison v. Gordon, 39 S.W.3d 604 (Tex. 2001): possession charges a purchaser with constructive notice only if it is 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal.' A tenant's occupancy of one unit in a fourplex was not exclusive or unequivocal, so it imparted no constructive notice and the purchaser won. The four-adjective test is a stricter, more defined formulation than the looser 'open and notorious' standard used elsewhere, so ambiguous or shared possession often fails to defeat BFP status in Texas.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Texas presumes fee simple passes unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (§5.001); §5.042 abolished Shelley's Case and worthier title but NOT destructibility.
§5.023 narrows the implied covenants of 'grant' to no-prior-conveyance + no-encumbrances — it does not imply seisin; the warranty comes from the express '§5.022' language.
A quitclaim conveys only the grantor's interest, is not a muniment of title, and its grantee is generally not a BFP — but §13.006 adds a four-year cleansing window for downstream purchasers.
Possession is constructive notice only if visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal (Madison v. Gordon).

flashcards.definition: A conveyance passes the entire fee simple estate unless the estate is limited by express words or a lesser estate is conveyed.
Only two: (1) grantor has not previously conveyed the estate, and (2) the estate is free from encumbrances. No implied covenant of seisin (ownership).
A quitclaim grantee is generally not a BFP (Woodward v. Ortiz), but a quitclaim recorded on/after Sept. 1, 2021 stops affecting good faith and stops being notice after its 4th anniversary (§13.006).
Possession imparts constructive notice to a purchaser only if it is 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal'; ambiguous/shared occupancy does not.

lessons.title: Boundary Retracement and Resolution Review
learning_objectives: State the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and explain that it yields to intent and the original surveyor's footsteps.
Apply Stafford v. King as the foundational Texas authority on calls and footsteps.
Distinguish sequential (senior/junior) from simultaneous (apportioned) conveyances and resolve excess/deficiency correctly.
Explain why proportionate measurement is a last resort behind found monuments in Texas.
key_takeaways: Dignity of calls: natural monuments > artificial monuments > (adjoinder) > course > distance > quantity — all subordinate to intent and footsteps.
Stafford v. King (1867) is the Texas origin of both the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the footsteps doctrine.
Sequential conveyances follow senior/junior rights (senior takes its full description; junior absorbs excess/deficiency); simultaneous conveyances apportion proportionately.
Found monuments and the original surveyor's footsteps control; proportionate measurement is a genuine last resort in Texas.

content.html:
Dignity of calls

When the calls in a single description conflict, Texas ranks them by relative reliability ('dignity'), highest to lowest: (1) natural monuments (rivers, creeks, lakes, mountains, rocks); (2) artificial monuments (marked trees, stakes, mounds, set iron, fences); (3) adjoinder (an adjacent, already-established survey line — a modern refinement inserted here); (4) course (bearing); (5) distance; and (6) quantity/area (weakest). Crucially, this hierarchy is only a rule of construction — a set of rebuttable reliability presumptions. It always yields to (i) the intent of the parties (paramount) and (ii) the footsteps of the original surveyor where they can be traced on the ground.

sections.title: Case: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867)
sections.content: The foundational Texas authority on boundary resolution. It establishes the dignity-of-calls hierarchy: 'Natural objects are mountains, lakes, rivers, creeks, rocks, and the like. Artificial objects are marked lines, trees, stakes, etc.' and that 'course and distance are the most unreliable calls; distance is less reliable than course.' It collapses the controlling order to '1, natural objects; 2, artificial objects; 3, course and distance' (the adjoinder tier and the split of course from distance are later refinements). Stafford also anchors the footsteps doctrine: 'The actual identification of the survey, the footsteps of the surveyor upon the ground, should always be followed, by whatever rule they may be traced.' And it frames the whole inquiry as choosing the construction 'most consistent with the intention apparent upon the face of the patent.'

sections.title: Case: Silver Oil & Gas, Inc. v. EOG Resources, Inc., 246 S.W.3d 197 (Tex. App.—San Antonio 2007)
sections.content: Restates the modern footsteps rule: 'the cardinal rule is that the footsteps of the original surveyor, if they can be ascertained, should be followed'; the actual lines and corners on the ground control even if inconsistent with the field notes. Only when those footsteps cannot be retraced does the surveyor fall back to the dignity-of-calls hierarchy (natural → artificial → course → distance). Compare TH Investments, Inc. v. Kirby Inland Marine, L.P., 218 S.W.3d 173 (Tex. App.—Houston [14th Dist.] 2007): the location of a survey line is ultimately a question of fact.

content.text: Exam tip: when a question gives a found, identified original monument that conflicts with a field-note course/distance, the monument (and the footsteps it evidences) controls — even over 'more accurate' modern measurements. The dignity hierarchy is a tiebreaker, not a trump card over intent and footsteps.

content.html:
Senior/junior rights and sequential vs. simultaneous conveyances

How you resolve excess or deficiency depends on how the parcels were created. In sequential conveyances (parcels carved from a larger tract one after another over time), senior/junior rights govern: the senior (earlier) deed takes the full measure of what its description grants, and the junior (later) deed takes only the remainder — the junior absorbs any excess or eats any deficiency. A grantor cannot convey land already conveyed to a senior grantee. Seniority does not expand a grant beyond its description; it only resolves overlaps and gaps in the senior's favor.

In simultaneous conveyances (multiple parcels created at one instant from a common scheme — classically, lots in a recorded subdivision plat), no lot is senior. Excess or deficiency found within the block is apportioned proportionately among the affected lots in proportion to their record lengths. The order in which the lots later sold is immaterial to seniority among them.

rows: Senior/junior rights: senior whole, junior takes the residue (no proration)
Lots in one recorded subdivision plat
Proportionate apportionment among lots by record length

content.html:
Proportionate measurement is a last resort

The PLSS/BLM single- and double-proportionate methods restore lost corners by distributing ordinary measurement error in proportion to record distances. But two cautions are heavily tested in Texas. First, proportioning applies only to simultaneously-created lots of equal dignity (and to restoring lost original corners) — never where senior rights govern. Second, and more important in Texas: found monuments and the original surveyor's footsteps control first. You proportion only where a corner is genuinely lost, not merely obliterated and recoverable from collateral evidence, and never to redistribute an isolable gross blunder (which is placed where it belongs).

content.text: Texas differs: single/double proportionate measurement are federal PLSS/BLM constructs — there is NO marquee Texas Supreme Court case adopting them by name. Because Texas land is held under GLO patents and original surveys rather than a uniform federal township grid, Texas retracement leans on locating the original surveyor's footsteps and monuments, and proportioning is genuinely a fallback. Do not attribute 'double proportionate measurement' to a famous Texas case.

content.alt: Diagram contrasting sequential conveyance with senior/junior cut-back versus simultaneous plat lots with proportionate apportionment of excess
content.caption: Figure: Sequential (senior/junior) vs. simultaneous (apportionment) resolution of excess and deficiency.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Dignity of calls: natural > artificial > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity — subordinate to intent (paramount) and the original surveyor's footsteps.
Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867) is the Texas origin of both the calls hierarchy and the footsteps doctrine.
Sequential = senior/junior (junior absorbs excess/deficiency, no proration); simultaneous = proportionate apportionment.
Found monuments and footsteps control; proportionate measurement is a last resort and is not adopted by name in any marquee Texas case.

flashcards.definition: Natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity/area — all subordinate to the parties' intent and the original surveyor's footsteps.
flashcards.hint_1: A rule of construction, not an absolute.

flashcards.definition: The actual footsteps of the original surveyor on the ground, where ascertainable, control the location of a line — even over inconsistent field-note calls or more accurate modern measurements.
Sequential: senior takes its full description, junior absorbs excess/deficiency (no proration). Simultaneous (plat lots): excess/deficiency apportioned proportionately; no lot is senior.
A PLSS/BLM method for lost corners and simultaneous lots; in Texas it is a last resort behind found monuments and footsteps, and no marquee Texas case adopts single/double proportioning by name.

lessons.title: Riparian and Water Rights Review
learning_objectives: Apply the Texas gradient boundary method and the statutory 30-foot navigability test.
Determine the seaward boundary of a coastal grant using the dual datum rule of Luttes v. State.
Explain State ownership of navigable streambeds and the effect of accretion, reliction, and avulsion.
Summarize the Open Beaches Act and the limit Severance v. Patterson placed on the rolling easement.
key_takeaways: The landward limit of a navigable streambed is the gradient boundary — a computed mid-bank line (Motl v. Boyd), not the ordinary high-water mark.
A stream is navigable if it retains an average bed width of 30 feet from its mouth up (Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3)).
Coastal datum depends on the sovereign: Spanish/Mexican grants use mean higher high water; Anglo/Texas grants use mean high tide (Luttes v. State).
Gradual change (accretion/erosion) moves the boundary; sudden change (avulsion) fixes it — and Severance limited the beach rolling easement after avulsion.

content.html:
Riparian vs. littoral; the gradient boundary

'Riparian' rights attach to land along a flowing watercourse; 'littoral' rights attach to land along a static body (lake, sea, Gulf). For a non-navigable stream the abutting owner's title runs to the center thread of the channel. For a navigable stream private title stops at the bank and the State owns the bed in trust for the public. The defining Texas peculiarity is where that bank line is: the gradient boundary. It is a graded line along the bank midway between (i) the lower water level that just reaches the cut bank and (ii) the higher level that just does not overtop it — refined as the mid-height point of the lowest qualified bank in the vicinity. It is a computed, surveyed line, not a simple observed high-water mark.

sections.title: Case: Motl v. Boyd, 116 Tex. 82, 286 S.W. 458 (1926)
sections.content: The Texas Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary as the line between public (State) and private ownership along navigable streams. It defined the 'bed' as the soil 'alternately covered and left bare … adequate to contain [the water] at its average and mean stage during an entire year, without reference to the extra freshets … or the extreme droughts.' Companion authorities: Oklahoma v. Texas, 260 U.S. 606 (1923) (the U.S. Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary for the Red River line; A.A. Stiles developed the method); Diversion Lake Club v. Heath, 126 Tex. 129 (1935); and Brainard v. State, 12 S.W.3d 6 (Tex. 2000) (leading modern application on the Canadian River, refining the mid-height-of-the-qualified-bank location).

sections.title: Case: Luttes v. State, 159 Tex. 500, 324 S.W.2d 167 (1959)
sections.content: The seaward boundary of upland depends on the sovereign source of the grant. A Spanish/Mexican grant is bounded by the line of mean higher high water (MHHW) — the average of the higher of the two daily high tides over a ~18.6-year tidal epoch — which pushes the boundary landward (larger State seabed). An Anglo-American / Republic- or State-of-Texas grant is bounded by the line of mean high tide / mean high water (the ordinary high-water mark). The difference flows from Spanish-Mexican civil law defining the 'shore' by the reach of the highest tides. The case concerned the 1829 Mexican 'Potrero de Buena Vista' grant on the Laguna Madre.

content.text: Texas differs: virtually every other state fixes the riparian boundary at the ordinary high-water mark and the tidal boundary at mean high tide universally (Borax v. Los Angeles). Texas uses a computed gradient line on streams and a DUAL coastal datum — MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Anglo/Texas grants. So a coastal surveyor must first trace the chain back to the original sovereign before choosing the datum.

content.html:
The 30-foot rule and State ownership of streambeds

Texas adds a bright-line geometric test of navigability. Under Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3), a 'navigable stream' is one that 'retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up.' The 30 feet is measured across the entire bed (bank to bank), not the water flowing on a given day — so a sometimes-dry channel can still be navigable. The rule originates in an 1837 act of the Republic. Because the State owns the beds of navigable streams in trust for the public, this geometric test can make even a small, non-commercial creek 'navigable' (and its bed State-owned) purely by width.

One curative overlay: the 1929 'Small Bill' relinquished the State's interest in the surface of certain streambeds to adjoining owners but reserved minerals to the State and preserved the public's rights in the waters.

content.text: Exam tip: the federal/common-law navigability test (The Daniel Ball) asks about navigability-in-fact for commerce. Texas SUPPLEMENTS that with the 30-foot average-bed-width test of §21.001(3) — found in essentially no other state. If a stem gives you an average bed width near 30 feet, the §21.001 test is the one being tested.

content.html:
Accretion, reliction, avulsion; Open Beaches

The controlling variable for moving water boundaries is gradual vs. sudden. Accretion (gradual addition of alluvion) and reliction (gradual uncovering by receding water) move the boundary — the owner gains land; erosion moves it landward and the owner loses land. Avulsion (a sudden, perceptible change — a flood cutting a new channel, a hurricane) fixes the boundary at its former location; title does not shift merely because the water suddenly moved.

On the coast, the Texas Open Beaches Act (Nat. Res. Code Ch. 61) guarantees the public a free right of access to and use of Gulf-front beaches and creates a prima facie presumption of a public easement on the dry beach between mean low tide and the vegetation line. Historically that easement 'rolled' landward as the vegetation line migrated.

sections.title: Case: Severance v. Patterson, 370 S.W.3d 705 (Tex. 2012)
sections.content: The Texas Supreme Court limited the rolling-easement doctrine. A public beach easement may gradually migrate with naturally shifting beaches, but it does NOT automatically 'roll' onto previously private dry land after an avulsive event (e.g., a hurricane that suddenly moves the vegetation line landward). After an avulsion the State must establish a new easement rather than rely on the prior one rolling inland. The decision imports the accretion/avulsion (gradual/sudden) boundary principle into beach-access law.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Navigable streambed limit = the computed gradient boundary (Motl v. Boyd); State owns the bed in trust; non-navigable streams run to the center thread.
§21.001(3): a stream is navigable if it retains an average bed width of 30 feet from the mouth up — a Texas-specific bright-line test.
Coastal datum is dual: Spanish/Mexican grants use mean higher high water; Anglo/Texas grants use mean high tide (Luttes v. State).
Gradual change moves the boundary; avulsion fixes it; Severance v. Patterson stops the beach easement from rolling onto private land after avulsion.

flashcards.definition: The landward limit of a navigable streambed: a graded line midway between the water level that just reaches the cut bank and the level that just fails to overtop it — a computed, surveyed line, not the ordinary high-water mark.
A 'navigable stream' is one that retains an average bed width of 30 feet from its mouth up; measured bank-to-bank, so a sometimes-dry channel can qualify.
Spanish/Mexican coastal grants are bounded by mean higher high water (MHHW); Anglo/Texas grants by mean high tide (MHW). Trace the sovereign before choosing the datum.

Gradual change (accretion/erosion) moves the boundary; sudden change (avulsion) fixes it. Severance v. Patterson (2012): the public beach easement does not roll onto private land after an avulsive event.

lessons.title: Texas Surveying Statutes — Deep Review
learning_objectives: Locate the controlling Texas codes for recording, adverse possession, easements, and surveyor practice.
Apply the pure-notice recording act and identify why a Texas BFP can win without recording first.
Distinguish the five adverse-possession limitation periods and the easement doctrines Texas favors and disfavors.
Cite the current TBPELS rule chapters and the surveyor liability limitations period.
key_takeaways: Texas is a PURE-NOTICE recording state: a later BFP without notice wins even if it records second or never (Prop. Code §13.001/§13.002).
Adverse possession lives in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16 (§§16.024–16.028), NOT the Property Code; periods are 3/5/10/25 years with distinct elements.
Texas disfavors prescriptive easements; joint/shared use defeats exclusivity (Tran v. Macha), and landlocked road access goes through necessity (Hamrick v. Ward).
Surveying standards are in 22 TAC Ch. 138 (Subch. E); Ch. 663 was repealed; suits against surveyors run under CPRC §16.011.

content.html:
The Texas recording act: a pure-notice state

Texas is a pure 'notice' jurisdiction. A subsequent purchaser (i) for valuable consideration (ii) without notice (actual or constructive) of a prior unrecorded conveyance takes free of it — and prevails even if the prior grantee records first, or the BFP never records at all. Recording by the BFP is not a condition of winning. The statutes: Prop. Code §13.001 makes an unrecorded conveyance 'void as to a creditor or to a subsequent purchaser for a valuable consideration without notice,' though binding on the parties and on anyone with notice; §13.002 makes a properly recorded instrument 'notice to all persons' (constructive notice); §11.001 directs recording in the county where the land lies.

content.text: Texas differs: the three regimes are race (first to record wins, even with notice), notice (later BFP without notice wins, recording order irrelevant), and race-notice (later BFP wins only if without notice AND records first). Texas is PURE NOTICE — the decisive point on the exam is that a Texas BFP without notice beats a prior unrecorded grantee even though the BFP records second or never. There is no recording-priority requirement for the BFP.

content.html:
Adverse possession: a wholly statutory, multi-track scheme

Texas adverse possession is entirely statutory and lives in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16, Subchapter B — not the Property Code (a frequent trap). Section 16.021 supplies the definitions: 'adverse possession' is 'an actual and visible appropriation of real property, commenced and continued under a claim of right that is inconsistent with and is hostile to the claim of another person.' Instead of one common-law test, Texas offers distinct limitation periods, each with its own elements; a claimant pleads under the period whose elements it can satisfy. The strongest paper basis earns the shortest period.

rows: Peaceable & adverse possession under title or color of title (strongest paper basis → shortest period)
Cultivate/use/enjoy + pay applicable taxes + claim under a duly registered deed (excludes quitclaim/forged); a single missed tax year is fatal
Peaceable & adverse, cultivating/using/enjoying; no deed/tax needed but capped at 160 acres absent enclosure or a recorded deed fixing boundaries
Runs regardless of a legal disability (minority, unsound mind) — absolute long-stop
Under a recorded instrument purporting to convey, even if void (excludes quitclaim/forged); extends to the whole area described

content.text: Texas differs: the §16.025 five-year track REQUIRES paying property taxes — a frequent claim-killer absent from traditional common law — and the §16.026 ten-year track caps the claim at 160 acres absent enclosure or a recorded boundary deed. §16.028 lets a recorded but VOID instrument support a 25-year claim, broader than common-law 'color of title.' Tacking across successive possessors in privity is allowed but the statutory prerequisites are stricter.

content.html:
Easements: what Texas favors and disfavors

Express easements (a writing satisfying the Statute of Frauds, Bus. & Com. Code §26.01) are the most-favored method in Texas. Easement by necessity requires (1) unity of ownership before severance, (2) strict necessity (true landlock, not mere convenience), and (3) necessity existing at severance; it terminates when the necessity ends. Implied easement by prior use requires unity, an open/apparent and continuous use, and reasonable necessity — but for roadway access to a landlocked parcel, Hamrick v. Ward, 446 S.W.3d 377 (Tex. 2014) channels the claim into necessity, not prior use. Prescriptive easements are disfavored: the decisive Texas requirement is exclusivity — use by the claimant and NOT by the owner. Joint or shared use defeats the claim.

sections.title: Case: Tran v. Macha, 213 S.W.3d 913 (Tex. 2006)
sections.content: Neighbors shared a driveway under a mutual mistake. The Texas Supreme Court reversed the prescriptive easement, holding that mutual or joint use does not support an exclusive, hostile claim. 'Joint use of a road, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement'; there must be an 'unmistakable assertion of a claim of exclusive ownership.' This is the key Texas departure from the general/Restatement view that, because an easement is non-possessory, exclusivity is not required for prescription. In Texas, shared use is normally fatal, so access disputes are funneled toward necessity easements and express grants.

content.html:
Surveyor practice statutes and liability

Surveyor licensing flows from Occupations Code Ch. 1071 (the Professional Land Surveying Practices Act), which defines the RPLS and LSLS credentials and authorizes discipline. The Board's administrative rules are in 22 TAC Part 6: Ch. 134 (licensing), Ch. 136 (firm registration), and Ch. 138 ('Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors'), whose professional and technical standards are in Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.) — including §138.83 (precision/accuracy), §138.87 (monumentation), and §138.91 (survey drawing/written description/report). The old surveying standards in 22 TAC Ch. 663 were repealed when the boards merged. The statute of limitations for suits against surveyors is in CPRC §16.011.

content.text: Texas differs / 2026 update: cite the CURRENT chapters. After HB 1523 (86th Leg., 2019) created TBPELS, the surveying standards moved from 22 TAC Ch. 663 to 22 TAC Ch. 138 (Subch. E). Any answer choice citing '§663' for surveying standards is outdated and wrong for 2026.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Pure-notice recording (§13.001/§13.002): a BFP without notice wins even recording second or never; record in the county where the land lies (§11.001).
Adverse possession = CPRC Ch. 16 (§§16.024–16.028), not the Property Code; periods 3/5/10/25 years, with tax payment required for the 5-yr track and a 160-acre cap on the 10-yr track.
Texas disfavors prescription — joint use defeats exclusivity (Tran v. Macha); landlocked road access goes through necessity (Hamrick v. Ward).
Surveyor licensing = Occupations Code Ch. 1071; current rules = 22 TAC Ch. 138 (Ch. 663 repealed); suits against surveyors = CPRC §16.011.

flashcards.definition: Pure notice (Prop. Code §13.001/§13.002): a subsequent purchaser for value without notice takes free of a prior unrecorded conveyance even if it records second or never. No recording-priority requirement for the BFP.

Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16, §§16.021–16.028 (NOT the Property Code). Five tracks: 3 yr (§16.024), 5 yr w/ taxes (§16.025), 10 yr / 160-acre cap (§16.026), 25 yr disability long-stop (§16.027), 25 yr void recorded instrument (§16.028).
Joint/shared use of a road cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement; Texas requires exclusivity (use by claimant, not the owner). Texas disfavors prescription.
22 TAC Part 6: Ch. 134 (licensing), Ch. 136 (firm registration), Ch. 138 (standards of practice, Subch. E §§138.81+). Ch. 663 repealed. Surveyor suits limitations: CPRC §16.011.

lessons.title: RPLS Practice Problems — Jurisprudence
learning_objectives: Solve scenario-based jurisprudence problems by identifying the controlling Texas authority.
Resolve boundary-conflict, conveyance, water, and recording fact patterns under Texas rules.
Compute the years required to perfect adverse possession on a given track.
Self-assess readiness against the high-yield clusters.
key_takeaways: Read the fact pattern for the trigger that selects the controlling rule (a found monument, a quitclaim, a coastal datum, a shared driveway).
When two answer choices look right, the correct one usually reflects the Texas-specific departure from general law.
For adverse possession, match the facts to the track whose elements are fully satisfied — the strongest paper basis gives the shortest period.
Justify every answer with a citation before you commit to it.

content.html:
How to work these problems

Each scenario below mirrors the style of the Texas jurisprudence exam: a short fact pattern with a single best answer. Work each one by (1) spotting the doctrine trigger in the facts, (2) naming the controlling authority, and (3) choosing the answer that reflects the Texas-specific rule. Use the embedded practice template to drill the one numeric skill that appears even on the jurisprudence side — the years required to perfect adverse possession on a given statutory track. The quiz questions attached to this module (see the quiz bank) supply the bulk of the scenario drills; this lesson frames the method and the self-check.

sections.title: Worked scenario 1 — conflicting calls
sections.content: Facts: A retracement finds the original surveyor's marked stone mound at a corner, 12 ft from where the field-note course and distance would place it. Which controls? Answer: the found original monument (and the footsteps it evidences) controls over the conflicting course/distance, under Stafford v. King and the footsteps doctrine. The dignity hierarchy (natural > artificial > course > distance) only confirms that an artificial monument outranks course and distance — but the deeper rule is that the original surveyor's actual footsteps govern.

sections.title: Worked scenario 2 — coastal datum
sections.content: Facts: You are locating the seaward boundary of a tract whose chain traces to an 1829 Mexican grant on the Laguna Madre. Which tidal datum? Answer: mean higher high water (MHHW), under Luttes v. State, because the grant is Spanish/Mexican. Had the chain traced to a Republic- or State-of-Texas grant, you would use mean high tide (MHW). Trace the sovereign first.

sections.title: Worked scenario 3 — quitclaim in the chain
sections.content: Facts: A purchaser pays value, has no actual knowledge of a prior unrecorded deed, but a quitclaim sits two links back, recorded in 2015. Is the purchaser a BFP? Answer: generally no for that 2015 quitclaim — a quitclaim is notice of possible title defects and its grantee/successors are generally not BFPs (Woodward v. Ortiz). Note the 2021 §13.006 cleansing window applies only to quitclaims recorded on/after Sept. 1, 2021, and only after four years; a 2015 quitclaim is not cleansed by §13.006.

content.text: Exam tip: many wrong answers are 'the general common-law rule.' If a choice states the nationally uniform rule and another states the Texas departure, and the facts are set in Texas, the Texas departure is almost always the intended answer (e.g., pure notice over race-notice; gradient boundary over high-water mark; exclusivity required for prescription).

content.html:
One numeric skill: years to perfect adverse possession

Even on a conceptual exam you may be asked how many more years a claimant needs to perfect title on a given track. The method is simple: identify the statutory period (3, 5, 10, or 25 years), confirm the elements for that track are met (color of title for §16.024; taxes + registered deed for §16.025; cultivation/use for §16.026), and subtract the years of peaceable possession already completed. The practice template below randomizes this.

content.alt: Decision flowchart: trace sovereign, identify doctrine trigger, name controlling authority, apply Texas-specific rule
content.caption: Figure: A four-step method for working Texas jurisprudence scenario questions.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Work each scenario by spotting the doctrine trigger, naming the controlling authority, and applying the Texas-specific rule.
When a choice states the general rule and another the Texas departure, the Texas departure is usually correct for a Texas-set fact pattern.
For adverse possession, match facts to the track whose elements are fully met, then subtract years already possessed from the statutory period.
A quitclaim recorded before Sept. 1, 2021 is not cleansed by §13.006.

flashcards.definition: Trace the sovereign (if relevant) → spot the doctrine trigger → name the controlling Texas authority → apply the Texas-specific rule.
Statutory period for the satisfied track (3/5/10/25) minus the years of peaceable possession already completed. The strongest paper basis (color of title) gives the shortest period (3 yr, §16.024).

lessons.title: RPLS Jurisprudence Practice Exam Tips
learning_objectives: Apply test-day tactics for the Texas jurisprudence examination.
Use a citation-first elimination strategy to choose between close answer choices.
Build a final-review checklist of the most-tested Texas departures.
Manage time and avoid the most common avoidable errors.
key_takeaways: Identify the Texas departure: the trap answer is usually the general common-law rule.
Eliminate by citation — if a choice cites a repealed or wrong authority (e.g., §663, or 'Property Code' for adverse possession), strike it.
Keep a one-page final-review list of high-yield departures and review it the night before.
Read every fact-pattern qualifier; small words (navigable, quitclaim, Spanish grant, before/after 2021) flip the answer.

content.html:
Test-day tactics

The jurisprudence exam rewards recognition speed: most questions are answerable in under a minute if you have pre-loaded the controlling authority for each doctrine. Read the stem twice and underline the qualifier that selects the rule — navigable vs. non-navigable stream, Spanish/Mexican vs. Texas grant, quitclaim vs. warranty deed, shared vs. exclusive use, recorded before or after Sept. 1, 2021. These small words flip the answer. Then choose the option that states the Texas-specific rule rather than the general common-law rule.

content.text: Exam tip — citation-first elimination: strike any choice that cites a wrong or repealed authority. 'Adverse possession under the Property Code' is wrong (it is in CPRC Ch. 16). 'Surveying standards under 22 TAC Ch. 663' is wrong (repealed; now Ch. 138). 'Double proportionate measurement adopted in [a named Texas case]' is wrong (no marquee Texas case adopts it by name).

content.html:
The high-yield departures to memorize cold

If you review nothing else the night before, review this list of the Texas departures most likely to decide a close question. Each is the place where choosing 'the general rule' is the trap.

  • Recording: Texas is pure notice — the BFP wins even recording second or never.
  • Adverse possession: in CPRC Ch. 16; the 5-yr track requires paying taxes; the 10-yr track caps at 160 acres.
  • Prescription: Texas disfavors it; exclusivity is required and joint use is fatal (Tran v. Macha).
  • Water (stream): gradient boundary, not the ordinary high-water mark (Motl v. Boyd); 30-ft navigability test (§21.001).
  • Water (coast): dual datum — MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Texas grants (Luttes).
  • Quitclaim/BFP: grantee generally not a BFP, but a 4-year cleansing window for post-2021 recordings (§13.006).
  • Standards: 22 TAC Ch. 138 (Ch. 663 repealed).

rows: BFP records after the prior grantee
Pure notice: BFP wins (no recording-priority requirement)
Claimant shared the driveway with the owner
No easement — joint use defeats exclusivity (Tran v. Macha)
Mean higher high water (MHHW) — Luttes v. State

content.text: Time management: do not over-invest in a single hard item. Flag it, choose the Texas-departure answer if two choices remain, and move on. Most jurisprudence points come from recognizing the controlling rule quickly across many questions, not from agonizing over one.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Underline the qualifier that selects the rule; small words (navigable, quitclaim, Spanish grant, before/after 2021) flip the answer.
Use citation-first elimination: strike choices citing repealed or wrong authority (§663; 'Property Code' for adverse possession).
Memorize the high-yield Texas departures cold — that is where close questions are decided.
Manage time: flag hard items, pick the Texas-departure answer between close choices, and keep moving.

flashcards.definition: Strike any answer that cites a repealed or wrong authority: '§663' for standards (now Ch. 138), 'Property Code' for adverse possession (it is CPRC Ch. 16), or a named Texas case for double proportionate measurement (none adopts it by name).
On a Texas-set fact pattern, the answer stating the general common-law rule is usually the trap; the answer stating the Texas departure is usually correct.

quiz_questions.question_text: A title examiner traces a South Texas tract back to a perfected 1831 Mexican league-and-labor grant in the GLO Spanish Collection. A later 'patent' purporting to grant the same land from the State of Texas appears in 1880. Which instrument controls the origin of title?
options: The 1880 State patent, because state patents always supersede colonial grants
The 1831 Mexican grant, because sovereignty over that soil was already severed and the land could not be re-patented
Whichever instrument was recorded first in the county records
The 1880 State patent, because Texas land became federal public domain at annexation
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The 1831 Mexican grant, because sovereignty over that soil was already severed and the land could not be re-patented
quiz_questions.explanation: A perfected Spanish/Mexican title severs the land from the sovereign and remains valid (protected by the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and the 1836 Republic Constitution). Land already conveyed by such a title cannot be re-patented, so the 1880 'patent' is a nullity as to that land. Texas land was never federal public domain.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does Texas use metes-and-bounds descriptions keyed to an original survey and abstract number rather than the PLSS township-range grid used in most western states?
options: Because the federal BLM never completed the rectangular survey of Texas
Because Texas retained its public lands at annexation in 1845, so its land was never federal public domain
Because the PLSS is prohibited by the Texas Constitution
Because Texas adopted the PLSS only for state-owned land
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because Texas retained its public lands at annexation in 1845, so its land was never federal public domain
quiz_questions.explanation: Under the 1845 Joint Resolution of Annexation, Texas retained all vacant and unappropriated public lands. Because the land was never federal public domain, the federal PLSS was never laid over it; Texas grants are metes-and-bounds tracts keyed to the original GLO survey plus abstract number.

quiz_questions.question_text: An old field note reads 'S 45° E, 950 vrs.' Using the Texas statutory vara, the distance in feet is approximately:
options: 950 ft
342 ft
quiz_questions.explanation: The Texas vara is statutorily 33⅓ in = 2.7778 ft. 950 × 2.7778 ≈ 2,638.9 ft. Do not apply a foreign (Spanish ~32.9 in or Mexican ~32.99 in) vara to a Texas grant.

quiz_questions.question_text: The General Land Office, custodian of Texas's original land-grant records and still issuing patents today, is a federal agency.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: The GLO is a Texas state agency, founded in 1836 — the oldest existing Texas state agency. Because Texas retained its public domain, the apex custodian is a state body, not the federal BLM.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Texas deed conveys land using the word 'grant' but contains no express warranty language. Under Prop. Code §5.023, which covenants does that word imply?
options: A covenant of seisin that the grantor actually owns the estate
Only that the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate and that it is free from encumbrances
A full general warranty against all defects in the chain of title
No covenants whatsoever, because 'grant' is merely descriptive
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Only that the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate and that it is free from encumbrances
quiz_questions.explanation: §5.023 narrows the common-law implied covenants of 'grant'/'convey' to just two: no prior conveyance and no encumbrances. It does NOT imply seisin (ownership). A general warranty in Texas comes from the express 'warrant and forever defend' language of §5.022.

quiz_questions.question_text: A purchaser pays full value in 2024 without actual notice of any defect. Two links back in the chain is a quitclaim deed recorded on October 1, 2018. Is the purchaser protected as a bona fide purchaser as to that quitclaim?
options: Yes — §13.006 cleanses every recorded quitclaim after four years
Generally no — the quitclaim is notice of possible title defects, and §13.006's cleansing window applies only to quitclaims recorded on or after Sept. 1, 2021
Yes — paying value automatically confers BFP status regardless of quitclaims
Generally no — a quitclaim in the chain permanently and irreversibly destroys BFP status in all cases
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Generally no — the quitclaim is notice of possible title defects, and §13.006's cleansing window applies only to quitclaims recorded on or after Sept. 1, 2021
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Woodward v. Ortiz a quitclaim grantee/successor is generally not a BFP. §13.006's four-year cleansing window applies only to quitclaims recorded on/after Sept. 1, 2021; a 2018 quitclaim is not cleansed, so the classic rule still governs. The 'permanently in all cases' option is wrong because §13.006 does soften the rule for post-2021 recordings.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Madison v. Gordon, a purchaser is charged with constructive notice from another's possession of the land only if that possession is:
options: Open and notorious
Visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal
Continuous for at least ten years
Accompanied by payment of property taxes
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal
quiz_questions.explanation: Madison v. Gordon (Tex. 2001) requires possession to be 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal' to impart constructive notice — a stricter, four-adjective test than the looser 'open and notorious' standard. A tenant's occupancy of one unit in a fourplex was not exclusive/unequivocal, so the purchaser took without notice.

quiz_questions.question_text: Prop. Code §5.042 abolished the doctrine of destructibility of contingent remainders in Texas.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: §5.042 abolished the Rule in Shelley's Case, the doctrine of worthier title, the rule forbidding a remainder to the grantor's heirs, and a homestead-lien rule — but NOT destructibility, which is not listed. Texas rejects destructibility through case law honoring grantor intent.

quiz_questions.question_text: During retracement, a surveyor locates the original surveyor's marked stone mound, which conflicts by 15 feet with the field-note distance. Under Stafford v. King and the footsteps doctrine, what controls?
options: The field-note distance, because modern measurements are more accurate
The found original monument, because the footsteps of the original surveyor control over conflicting course and distance
The stated quantity/acreage, which is the most reliable call
An average of the monument position and the field-note distance
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The found original monument, because the footsteps of the original surveyor control over conflicting course and distance
quiz_questions.explanation: Stafford v. King establishes that the footsteps of the original surveyor, where ascertainable, control. An artificial monument also outranks course and distance in the dignity hierarchy, and quantity is the weakest call — but the governing principle is following the original surveyor's actual work on the ground.

quiz_questions.question_text: Rank the dignity of calls from highest to lowest as applied in Texas after Stafford and modern refinement.
options: Distance > course > artificial monuments > natural monuments > quantity
Natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity
Quantity > distance > course > artificial monuments > natural monuments
Course > distance > natural monuments > artificial monuments > quantity
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity
quiz_questions.explanation: The dignity hierarchy runs natural monuments, then artificial monuments, then adjoinder (a modern refinement), then course, then distance, then quantity/area (weakest). It is a rebuttable rule of construction subordinate to intent and the original surveyor's footsteps.

quiz_questions.question_text: A grantor sold Parcel A in 2005 and Parcel B (the remainder of the parent tract) in 2010. A resurvey finds the parent tract is 8 feet short of the combined record dimensions along the common boundary. How is the deficiency resolved?
options: Proportion the 8-foot shortage between Parcel A and Parcel B by their record lengths
Make senior Parcel A whole to its full description; junior Parcel B absorbs the entire 8-foot deficiency
Split the deficiency equally, 4 feet each, regardless of seniority
The deficiency is ignored because area is the least reliable call
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Make senior Parcel A whole to its full description; junior Parcel B absorbs the entire 8-foot deficiency
quiz_questions.explanation: These are sequential conveyances, so senior/junior rights govern: the senior (Parcel A, conveyed first) takes its full description and the junior (Parcel B) absorbs the deficiency. Proportionate apportionment applies to simultaneous conveyances (plat lots of equal dignity), not sequential carve-outs.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surveyor testifies that he located a disputed corner by 'double proportionate measurement, as adopted by the Texas Supreme Court.' What is the flaw in that statement?
options: Double proportionate measurement is unconstitutional in Texas

Single and double proportionate measurement are federal PLSS/BLM constructs, and no marquee Texas case adopts them by name; in Texas, proportioning is a last resort behind found monuments and footsteps
Proportionate measurement may only be used on coastal boundaries
There is no flaw; the Texas Supreme Court adopted the BLM method by name in Brainard v. State
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Single and double proportionate measurement are federal PLSS/BLM constructs, and no marquee Texas case adopts them by name; in Texas, proportioning is a last resort behind found monuments and footsteps
quiz_questions.explanation: Proportionate measurement comes from the federal BLM Manual for restoring lost PLSS corners. Texas, holding land under GLO patents rather than a uniform federal grid, leans on the original surveyor's footsteps and monuments; proportioning is a fallback used only where a corner is genuinely lost. Brainard is a gradient-boundary case, not a proportioning adoption.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a recorded subdivision, all lots were created simultaneously by the plat. A resurvey finds a 3-foot surplus across a block of equal-record-length lots. How is the surplus distributed?
options: The lot that sold first takes the entire surplus as the senior lot
The surplus is apportioned proportionately among the lots by their record lengths, because no lot is senior
The last lot to sell absorbs the surplus
The surplus reverts to the original subdivider
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The surplus is apportioned proportionately among the lots by their record lengths, because no lot is senior
quiz_questions.explanation: Plat lots are a simultaneous conveyance, so no lot is senior and the order of later sales is immaterial. Excess or deficiency within the block is apportioned proportionately by record length. Senior/junior rules apply only to sequential conveyances.

quiz_questions.question_text: Along a navigable Texas stream, the boundary between the State-owned bed and the private upland is located using:
options: The ordinary high-water mark identified by a vegetation line
The gradient boundary — a computed line at the mid-height of the qualified (cut) bank (Motl v. Boyd)
The thread (center) of the stream
The line of mean higher high water
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The gradient boundary — a computed line at the mid-height of the qualified (cut) bank (Motl v. Boyd)
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas uses the gradient boundary, a graded line midway between the water level that just reaches the cut bank and the level that just fails to overtop it (Motl v. Boyd). Most states use the ordinary high-water mark; the thread rule is for non-navigable streams; MHHW is a coastal datum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3), a Texas stream is 'navigable' if it:
options: Carries flowing water year-round
Retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up, measured across the entire bed
Is actually used for interstate commerce
Has a drainage area exceeding one square mile
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up, measured across the entire bed
quiz_questions.explanation: §21.001(3) supplies a bright-line geometric test: an average bed width of 30 feet from the mouth up, measured bank-to-bank (not the water on a given day). A sometimes-dry channel can therefore be navigable. The commerce test is the federal/common-law standard, which Texas supplements.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surveyor must set the seaward boundary of a tract whose chain of title originates in a Republic-of-Texas grant. Which tidal datum applies under Luttes v. State?
options: Mean higher high water (MHHW)
Mean high tide / mean high water (MHW)
Mean low water
The vegetation line
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Mean high tide / mean high water (MHW)
quiz_questions.explanation: Luttes v. State sets a dual datum keyed to the sovereign source: Spanish/Mexican grants use mean higher high water (landward), while Anglo-American Republic/State grants use mean high tide (MHW). A Republic-of-Texas grant therefore uses MHW.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hurricane suddenly moves the vegetation line landward across what had been private dry beach. Under Severance v. Patterson, does the public beach easement automatically 'roll' onto that newly landward private land?
options: Yes — the rolling easement always migrates with the vegetation line
No — after an avulsive event the easement does not automatically roll; the State must establish a new easement
Yes — but only if the State pays compensation
No — the Open Beaches Act was repealed by Severance
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No — after an avulsive event the easement does not automatically roll; the State must establish a new easement
quiz_questions.explanation: Severance v. Patterson limited the rolling easement: it may migrate with gradual, natural change, but it does not automatically roll onto previously private land after a sudden avulsive event. The State must then establish a new easement. The Act was not repealed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gradual accretion adds soil to a riparian tract over decades, while a separate flood suddenly cuts a new channel. Which statement correctly states the boundary consequences?
options: Both the accretion and the avulsion move the boundary with the water
Accretion moves the boundary (owner gains land); the avulsion fixes the boundary at its former location
Accretion fixes the boundary; the avulsion moves it
Neither changes the boundary because water boundaries are always fixed
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Accretion moves the boundary (owner gains land); the avulsion fixes the boundary at its former location
quiz_questions.explanation: The controlling variable is gradual vs. sudden. Gradual accretion (and reliction) moves the boundary so the owner gains land; sudden avulsion fixes the boundary at its former location regardless of the new water line.

quiz_questions.question_text: A prior grantee receives a deed in 2019 but does not record it. In 2020 the same grantor conveys the land to a purchaser for value who has no notice of the 2019 deed; that purchaser records in 2021, after the 2019 grantee finally records in 2020. Under Texas law, who prevails?
options: The 2019 grantee, because that deed was first in time and recorded before the purchaser
The 2020 purchaser, because Texas is a pure-notice state and a BFP without notice prevails even if it records second
The 2019 grantee, because Texas is a race-notice state and the first to record wins
Neither — the conflicting deeds cancel and title reverts to the grantor
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The 2020 purchaser, because Texas is a pure-notice state and a BFP without notice prevails even if it records second
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas is a pure-notice jurisdiction (Prop. Code §13.001/§13.002). A subsequent purchaser for value without notice takes free of the prior unrecorded conveyance even though the prior grantee records first. There is no recording-priority requirement for the BFP, so the 'race-notice' answer is the trap.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which Texas code does the law of adverse possession reside?
options: The Texas Property Code
The Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code, Chapter 16
The Texas Natural Resources Code, Chapter 21
The Texas Occupations Code, Chapter 1071
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code, Chapter 16
quiz_questions.explanation: Adverse possession is in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16, §§16.021–16.028 — a common trap, because students assume the Property Code. The Natural Resources Code Ch. 21 covers surveys/navigability; Occupations Code Ch. 1071 covers surveyor licensing.

quiz_questions.question_text: A claimant has openly cultivated and used a tract under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed and has paid all property taxes for six years, but missed paying taxes in one of those years. Which statement is correct about the five-year adverse-possession track (§16.025)?
options: Title is perfected because six years exceeds the five-year period
The claim under §16.025 fails because tax payment is a hard requirement and a single missed year is fatal
The missed year is excused if the claimant later pays it with interest
Tax payment is irrelevant to any adverse-possession track in Texas
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The claim under §16.025 fails because tax payment is a hard requirement and a single missed year is fatal
quiz_questions.explanation: The §16.025 five-year track requires cultivation/use, a duly registered deed, AND payment of applicable taxes; a single missed year breaks the requirement and is fatal to that track. (The claimant might still pursue the ten-year §16.026 track, which needs no taxes but caps at 160 acres absent enclosure or a recorded boundary deed.)

quiz_questions.question_text: Two neighbors shared a single gravel driveway for over twenty years under a mutual mistake about the boundary, both using it regularly. One now claims a prescriptive easement. Under Tran v. Macha, will the claim succeed?
options: Yes — twenty years of continuous use satisfies the prescriptive period
No — joint or shared use cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement because Texas requires exclusivity
Yes — mutual mistake makes the use hostile
No — prescriptive easements require payment of taxes like adverse possession
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No — joint or shared use cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement because Texas requires exclusivity
quiz_questions.explanation: Tran v. Macha holds that joint use of a road, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement; Texas requires an unmistakable assertion of exclusive ownership. Texas disfavors prescription, so shared use is normally fatal — unlike the general common-law/Restatement view that does not require exclusivity for a non-possessory easement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which citation correctly identifies the current source of the Texas surveyor standards of practice for 2026?
options: 22 TAC Chapter 663
22 TAC Chapter 138 (Subchapter E)
Texas Property Code Chapter 138
Occupations Code Chapter 663
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 22 TAC Chapter 138 (Subchapter E)
quiz_questions.explanation: After HB 1523 (2019) created TBPELS, the surveying standards moved to 22 TAC Ch. 138, with the professional and technical standards in Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.). The old 22 TAC Ch. 663 was repealed; any 2026 citation to §663 is outdated.

quiz_questions.question_text: A landlocked owner whose parcel was once part of a single unified tract seeks a roadway easement for access. Under Hamrick v. Ward, which doctrine must the claim proceed under?
options: Implied easement by prior use, using the 'reasonably necessary' standard
Easement by necessity, requiring strict necessity
Prescriptive easement, requiring ten years of use
Express easement only — no easement by operation of law is available
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Easement by necessity, requiring strict necessity
quiz_questions.explanation: Hamrick v. Ward (Tex. 2014) channels roadway-access claims to a landlocked, previously unified parcel into easement-by-necessity (strict necessity), not the easier prior-use 'reasonably necessary' standard. Prior use still applies to non-access uses like utility lines.

quiz_questions.question_text: A claimant has held peaceable, adverse possession under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed, cultivating the land and paying all taxes, for 2 full years. Under the strongest applicable track for which all elements are met, how many MORE years are needed to perfect title?
quiz_questions.explanation: With a registered deed plus taxes the claimant qualifies under the five-year track (§16.025). 5 − 2 = 3 more years. (If only color of title applied, the three-year §16.024 track would already be satisfied; but the question asks under the track whose elements — registered deed + taxes — are stated, which is the five-year track.)

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal surveyor is told only that the seaward boundary follows 'mean higher high water.' Without seeing the deed, what does that datum tell you about the likely sovereign source of the grant?
options: It is an Anglo-American Republic- or State-of-Texas grant
It is a Spanish or Mexican grant, because those use mean higher high water under Luttes v. State
It must be a federal patent
The datum is unrelated to the sovereign source
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is a Spanish or Mexican grant, because those use mean higher high water under Luttes v. State
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Luttes v. State the MHHW datum is used for Spanish/Mexican grants (the civil-law 'highest tides' shore), while Anglo/Texas grants use mean high tide. So an MHHW boundary signals a civil-law sovereign source.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a Texas-set fact pattern, two answer choices remain: one states the general common-law rule and one states a Texas-specific departure. As a test-taking heuristic, which is usually the intended answer?
options: The general common-law rule, because it is more widely accepted
The Texas-specific departure, because the jurisprudence exam tests where Texas differs
Always the longest answer choice
Always the choice that cites a federal statute
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The Texas-specific departure, because the jurisprudence exam tests where Texas differs
quiz_questions.explanation: The Texas jurisprudence exam concentrates on the points where Texas departs from general common law, so the trap answer is usually the general rule and the intended answer is the Texas departure (pure notice, gradient boundary, exclusivity for prescription, adverse possession in the CPRC, etc.). Answer length and federal citations are not reliable signals.

quiz_questions.question_text: An answer choice states that 'the standard of care for a Texas boundary survey is governed by 22 TAC Chapter 663.' Using citation-first elimination, what should you do?
options: Select it, because Chapter 663 is the long-standing surveying standard
Eliminate it, because Chapter 663 was repealed and the standards are now in 22 TAC Chapter 138
Select it only if no case is cited
Eliminate it because standards are never found in the Administrative Code
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Eliminate it, because Chapter 663 was repealed and the standards are now in 22 TAC Chapter 138
quiz_questions.explanation: Citation-first elimination: strike choices that cite repealed or wrong authority. The surveying standards moved from 22 TAC Ch. 663 to Ch. 138 after the 2019 board merger, so a 2026 reference to §663 is outdated. (Standards do properly live in the Administrative Code, so the last option is wrong.)

problem_templates.name: Years remaining to perfect adverse possession (color-of-title / 3-year track)
problem_templates.question_template: A claimant has held peaceable and adverse possession under color of title, satisfying the elements of the Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code §16.024 three-year track, for {{held}} full years. How many more years of continued peaceable possession are required to perfect title under that track?
problem_templates.explanation_template: The §16.024 track (peaceable & adverse possession under title or color of title) is a 3-year period — the strongest paper basis earns the shortest period. With {{held}} years completed, {{_answer}} more years are required.

images_needed.caption: Timeline of the three-year color-of-title adverse-possession track
